svg.title: N-gram backoff hierarchy
svg.desc: A trigram model first tries to estimate the next-word probability from the two-word history. Because the count table is sparse, most specific contexts were never seen, so when the trigram count is zero the model backs off to the shorter bigram context, and if that is also unseen it backs off again to the unigram, which always has a count. Each level keeps a discounted mass reserved for backing off, and Kneser-Ney smoothing replaces raw counts with a continuation probability that estimates how likely a word is to appear in a new context, rather than how often it appeared historically.

g.text: Ch. 2 — falling back to shorter histories when a specific context was never observed
always has a count — floor of the hierarchy
no → count found, stop backing off

text.tspan: Instead of “how often did this word occur”, estimate
“in how many distinct contexts could this word
continue a phrase” — fixes over-counting of words
(e.g. “Francisco”) that are frequent but not versatile.

g.text: backoff — context unseen, fall to shorter n-gram
zero / unobserved cell in count table
Sparsity (Ch.2.2) means most specific contexts are hatch-marked zero cells above.
